coredata.dc:title: A sustainable alternative to synthesis optical sensing receptor for the detection of metal ions
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2014 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Tiong, Adden Nguong Leong
Wong, Nicholas Kwong Hui
Fong, Jessica Fung Yee
Tan, Xian Wen
Ng, Sing Muk
coredata.dc:description: The development of new optical sensing receptors not only need to focus on its sensitivity and selectivity aspects, but should also emphasis on the sustainability of the development as a whole. This report proposed a green synthesis method to produce fluorescent carbon nanoparticles via thermal carbonisation of orange peels. This is a sustainable option since used agricultural waste as starting precursor, involved no excess or toxic chemical reagents, produced low or no waste side product, and employed simple synthesis steps. Parameters governing the precursor conversion into carbon rich residues were investigated and found that the optimum carbonisation occurs at the temperature range of 300–350°C for an exposure time of 2h. The carbon residue was then grinded into fine nanoparticles, dispersed in water and followed by sonication to promote better dispersion in water. Colloidal suspension remaining in the aliquot after centrifugation for 15min at 13,400rpm was collected and found to show strong fluorescence emission at 435nm, when excited at the optimum wavelength of 325nm. This unique optical property has been utilised for sensing application since the fluorescence intensity was significantly quenched in the presence of heavy metal ions. Analytical characteristic was evaluated using standard Stern–Volmer equation and the limit of detection was evaluated to be significantly low that enable the practical utilisation for quantification applications. It can serve as a less toxic fluorophore candidate to replace some of those existing quantum dots or dyes that are less sustainable in nature or in terms of its development approach.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon nanoparticles
Orange peels
Fluorescence
Quenching
Optical sensing
Metal ions

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Optimisation of synthetic and analytical variables
Sensing potential of heavy metal ions

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) Typical photoluminescence spectra excited at different excitation wavelengths and the UV–vis absorption for CNPs and control. (B) Photograph taken under UV for CNPs (left) and control (right).
The FTIR recorded for the (A) orange peel and (B) CNPs.

(A) Fluorescence intensity of the supernatants isolated from residues after carbonised at different temperatures with standing time of 2h. (B) The kinetic of the carbonisation process under the pyrolysis temperature of 325°C.
The intensities of the isolated supernatants after different centrifugation times.
The intensity of the CNPs under different pH conditions.
The quenching effect on the CNPs in the presence of different metal ions at various concentrations.

The normalised fluorescence signals decay of CNPs in the presence of Hg(II) and Sn(II) ions at an increasing concentration. Insert is the plots of standard Stern–Volmer relationship for the quenching trends of CNPs with the respective ions.
Summary of the analytical characteristics of the carbon nanoparticles with various metal ions tested in this study.

head.ce:title: A sustainable alternative to synthesis optical sensing receptor for the detection of metal ions

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Faculty of Engineering, Computing, and Science, Swinburne University of Technology Sarawak Campus, Jalan Simpang Tiga, 93350 Kuching, Sarawak, Malaysia

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Faculty of Engineering, Computing, and Science
Swinburne University of Technology Sarawak Campus

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Swinburne Sarawak Research Centre for Sustainable Technologies, Swinburne University of Technology Sarawak Campus, Jalan Simpang Tiga, 93350 Kuching, Sarawak, Malaysia

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Swinburne Sarawak Research Centre for Sustainable Technologies
Swinburne University of Technology Sarawak Campus

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Faculty of Engineering, Computing, and Science, Swinburne University of Technology Sarawak Campus, Jalan Simpang Tiga, 93350 Kuching, Sarawak, Malaysia. Tel.: +60 82 260848; fax: +60 82 260813.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The development of new optical sensing receptors not only need to focus on its sensitivity and selectivity aspects, but should also emphasis on the sustainability of the development as a whole. This report proposed a green synthesis method to produce fluorescent carbon nanoparticles via thermal carbonisation of orange peels. This is a sustainable option since used agricultural waste as starting precursor, involved no excess or toxic chemical reagents, produced low or no waste side product, and employed simple synthesis steps. Parameters governing the precursor conversion into carbon rich residues were investigated and found that the optimum carbonisation occurs at the temperature range of 300–350°C for an exposure time of 2h. The carbon residue was then grinded into fine nanoparticles, dispersed in water and followed by sonication to promote better dispersion in water. Colloidal suspension remaining in the aliquot after centrifugation for 15min at 13,400rpm was collected and found to show strong fluorescence emission at 435nm, when excited at the optimum wavelength of 325nm. This unique optical property has been utilised for sensing application since the fluorescence intensity was significantly quenched in the presence of heavy metal ions. Analytical characteristic was evaluated using standard Stern–Volmer equation and the limit of detection was evaluated to be significantly low that enable the practical utilisation for quantification applications. It can serve as a less toxic fluorophore candidate to replace some of those existing quantum dots or dyes that are less sustainable in nature or in terms of its development approach.

ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescence technique has been widely employed in bioassays and optical sensors. This mainly due to its capability to achieve extremely low detection limit, where there are increasing examples demonstrating the detection limit down to single-molecule level [1–3]. Besides, the principles underlying fluorescence spectroscopy are well understood, making it easy to be utilised as an analytical probe. Generally, fluorescence technique consists of several main operating components, which include an excitation source, waveguides, fluorophores, and a photo-detector. In a typical working scheme, the interaction of an analyte with the fluorophore will subsequently cause a recordable change in the property of fluorescence emission. The unknown concentration of the analyte can be later evaluated from a standard calibration curve [4]. Usually, the most challenging task in developing a new fluorescence probe is on obtaining a suitable fluorophore that can detect a targeted analyte. The instrumentation of the probe is less of an effort since the electronic and mechanical components are readily available from the market with various specifications and most of them can be directly ‘plugged and play’ on a computer.

Fluorophores are usually extracted from natural resources such as directly from plants in the early days, but often this has some limitations for sensing tasks due to the lack of specificity. Later, advancement has progressed into the option to synthesis organic fluorescent dyes in laboratories. This gives more flexibility as the engineering of the fluorophores with specific chemical functionalities is now made possible. Cyanine- and azo-based dyes and its respective derivatives are some common examples of fluorophores that have been popularly synthesised in-house for optical sensing applications [5–9]. Although these dyes serve well for sensing with simple sensing mechanism, the synthesis process is often tedious, uses of various reagents and chemicals, and produces side products that some could be toxic and harmful to the environment and health. In addition, purification requires large amount of solvent that is costly and non-sustainable. More recently, the discovery of quantum dots has made another alternative for fluorophore. These semiconductor nanoparticles show good photostability, high quantum yield, chemically stable, and having tuneable emission [10], which have been successfully adopted for fluorescent labelling and sensing [11–14]. Although the performance is good, likewise, the synthesis process suffers similar limitations as faced by their dyes counterpart. In addition, there is also concern over its toxicity impact since part of the building blocks is of heavy metal that could leach out during the application stage.

It is clear that current technology could produce various types of fluorophores with specific analytical characteristics, but majority of synthesis processes have neglected the aspect of sustainability. This is associated especially on the use of large amount of chemicals, toxicity of the reagents, and wastes generated from the synthesis that could have negative impact to the environment and living organisms including human. To strike a counterbalance, the synthesis process should also emphasis on minimising or eliminating totally the use of toxic reagents and to eliminate the production of side products from the synthesis. In view of this, the green analytical chemistry approach should be emphasised more, which defined generally as the use of chemistry with the intention for pollution prevention [15]. Go green is one of the most recent trends in community of science and technology in the effort to promote sustainability in ensuring the future generation can enjoy what the world is like today. More and more studies have reported examples of adopting greener approach in their works, for instance in extraction [16], catalyst [17], polymer technology [18], and organic synthesis [19].

In this work, a green approach has been taken to synthesis fluorophore using agricultural waste of orange peels, without producing toxic side products along the process. Orange peels have been chosen since it is considered waste, a renewable source with no other known potential usage, and can be easily obtained all year round from tropical countries including Malaysia. The orange peels were carbonised using simple thermal pyrolysis in an oven before grinded into fine carbon nanoparticles (CNPs) with fluorescence property. The fluorescent CNPs were then employed as luminescent probe for the detection of various metal ions based on its quenching trend. This work is definitely greener since adopting waste as starting precursor, using less synthesis procedure and reagent, simple since using only thermal carbonisation, clean as it produces no toxic or harmful side products, and finally giving comparable sensing characteristic to those dyes and quantum dots. This work serves as one of the novel examples on using sustainable alternative in the area of optical sensor development.

All chemicals used in this study were of analytical grade unless otherwise stated. Ultrapure water (∼18.2MΩ,25°C) was obtained from a Milipore Mili Q-system and used as solvents throughout the study. Peels of green orange (Pericarpium Citri Reticulatae Viride) was obtained from local market at Kuching, Sarawak, which was carefully washed with running water and rinse with double distilled deionised water before used. Stock solutions of metal ions were prepared from its respective salts, (Cu(NO3)2, SnCl2, Ni(NO3)2, Al(NO3)3, Co(NO3)2, Pb(NO3)2, and HgCl2) that were purchased from R&M marketing, Malaysia and used without further purification.

Fluorescence intensity was recorded using fluorescence spectrofluorometer (CARY Eclipse, Varian) set under fluorescence mode with both the excitation and emission slits of 5nm. Sample was placed in a 4-sides polished quartz cuvette with the path length of 10mm. Laboratory furnace (Carbolite ELF 11/14 B) with a maximum setting temperature of 1100°C was used for carbonisation. Sonicator (Branson 5510 Ultrasonic cleaner) was used for sample dispersion and centrifuge system (Eppendorf Minispin) was used for the separation of bulk residue from the aliquot that was rich in CNPs in the dispersed form. FTIR was recorded using IR spectrometer (Shimadzu).

The preparation of the CNPs is mainly divided into two major steps; carbonisation of orange peels via thermal approach and isolation of fine nanoparticles through centrifugation. Fresh orange was obtained from the local market and the skin was carefully peeled and cut into the rough dimension of 10mm2. These peels were washed under running water and rinsed with distilled water before left to dry in an oven for overnight. For carbonisation, approximate 5.0g of the orange peels were placed into a porcelain crucible and place into a furnace. The peels were heated from room temperature to a final pre-set temperature (ranging from 250°C to 450°C) and kept at the set temperature for 2h. Sample was removed from the furnace after that and cooled to room temperature in a desiccator. The black residue obtained was grinded into fine powder using pestle and mortar. In order to separate the fine nanoparticles from the bulkier particles, 0.01g of the grinded product was dispersed into 10ml of ultrapure water and homogenised under sonication for an hour. Following that, the mixture was centrifuged at 13,400rpm for 10min and the slightly yellowish supernatant was collected, leaving the non-soluble residue at the bottom of the tube. The supernatant was kept inside a sample bottle, sealed and stored at cold dark place for further analyses.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimisation of synthetic and analytical variables
ce:section.ce:para: Synthetic parameters affecting the final fluorescence of the CNPs were investigated. The optimum temperature used for carbonisation of the orange peels was determined by altering the temperature of the furnace from 250 to 400°C and the standing period ranging from 1 to 4h for each of the fixed temperature. The fluorescence intensities of the final obtained samples were monitored at 435nm with the excitation wavelength that was set at 325nm. The effect of time taken for sonication and centrifugation against the fluorescence intensity were also investigated. All standard procedures involved in the optimisation were set at similar condition and setting except the parameter under investigation.

The potential of CNPs as sensing probe for metal ions detection was investigated. Different metal ion solutions (made from Cu(NO3)2, SnCl2, Ni(NO3)2, Co(NO3)2, Pb(NO3)2, AgNO3 and HgCl2) were added into the CNPs solution and any alteration and change on the emission spectrum will be recorded. To investigate the analytical trend, dependency of the signal change on the alteration of the concentration of the metal ions was studied by adding increasing amount of the metal ions to the CNPs sample. For quantification and data analysis purposes, all intensity data recorded were first normalised against the blank, where the blank consisted only the fluorescent CNPs without addition of metal ions. The limit of detection (LOD) of the CNPs towards different metal ions was evaluated using the standard 3σ/s, where σ is the standard deviation of blank (n =6), while the s is he slope of the calibration of the technique obtained from the plot of intensity versus concentration.

This study emphasises a green synthesis approach of producing fluorophore from agricultural waste of orange peels using a simple and cost effective thermal pyrolysis. The product isolated at the end of the synthesis found to show significant fluorescence, as compared to the starting precursor of the peels that showed no or low fluorescence. The original green colour peel was observed to have change in its physical appearance to black residue after the pyrolysis process. This showed the formation of carbon due to pyrolysis. Agricultural wastes often ended up with three major products after pyrolysis; solid char, liquid and gas products [20]. The yield and composition depend very much on the pyrolysis condition. The decomposition of orange peel usually occurred in stages as the temperature being raised as reported by Miranda et al. [20]. This was corresponding to the decomposition of water soluble components, hemicellulose, cellulose and lignin, respectively. In this study, the liquid and gas products were not trapped and so the finally obtained residue will be the solid char, which was basically carbon rich residue. This has been further affirmed with the yield of residue obtained from this study that was recorded at around 25.0±0.5w/w %, which is matching with the value report by Miranda et al. (2009) [20].

In this study, the directly obtained black residue after pyrolysis showed low and no fluorescence under the UV light. Fluorescence was only observed from the supernatant collected after the centrifugation of the solution containing the grinded black carbon residue. This observation of fluorescence has confirmed the formation of CNPs, since carbon only fluoresces when in nano-sized dimension [21,22]. The possibility of the fluorescence origin from other organic compounds within the peel was low as the temperature applied during the pyrolysis can decompose these organic compounds. Besides, there was no observation of fluorescence from the supernatant collected from the solution after soaking the peels without pyrolysis for 2h, which further reduce the possibility of fluorescence that originated from possible organic compounds. Therefore, the observation of fluorescence must have come from the physically engineered carbon in the nanoparticles format.

The CNPs isolated was slightly yellowish but clear when dispersed in water. To confirm the fluorescence, the spectra of the obtained CNPs were recoded using a standard laboratory spectrofluorometer as shown in Fig. 1 A. It was observed that the CNPs gave a strong emission band, peaking at 435nm when excited at the wavelength of 325nm. The excitation profile monitored for the emission at 435nm clearly indicated that the CNPs can be excited by broad excitation energy. It was able to maintain at least 65% of the maximum emission intensity with any excitation wavelength studied, ranging from 250 to 350nm. This broad range allows simultaneous excitation using single excitation source for the CNPs along with another fluorophores that have even narrow excitation energy, as long as it falls within the range. The control was taken as the solution isolate after soaking the peels without pyrolysis and showed no obvious fluorescence. This was further supported with the bright blue glow emission by the CNPs when placed under UV light, as compared to the control that remained dull (Fig. 1B).

In view of CNPs, the emission band was often observed to be size dependent and some have shifted accordingly when excited at different energies [23,24]. However, the origin of the emission has not being well understood and still requires further clarification. There have been attempts to explain the origin by doing analogy comparison with the quantum dots and silicon nanoparticles, and the results suggested that the most likely mechanism will be the surface defects [25,26], although some did suggested the band-gap like transition due to size confinement [23]. This makes it possible to relate the emission property as a variable that reflects on the homogeneity of the fluorescence origin. Narrower emission band can imply better homogeneity of the fluorescence origin regardless if it was from the size effect or surface defect, while wider band explains the opposite. Thus the full width at half maximum (FWHM) of the emission band will be a good indicator to evaluate on the homogeneity of the CNPs. In this study, the FWHM of the fluorescence spectrum was evaluated to be around 100nm (Fig. 1A), which was considered narrow and/or similar to those reported elsewhere [27,28]. This will be of advantage especially in later applications to obtain better consistency of the fluorescence signals. The UV–vis absorption spectrum for the CNPs gave a broad peak around 200–300nm, which could be attributed to the electron transition of π−π∗ and n−π∗ for the CC and CO bonds of the oxygen-containing groups.

The FTIR spectrum of the orange peel displayed a number of adsorption peaks that indicating the presence of complex combination of organic moieties, while the CNPs showed smoother spectrum (Fig. 2 ). This could be due to the high temperature that decomposed most of the organic moieties during pyrolysis. For instance, the strong absorption at 2920, 2852 and 1637cm−1 attributed to CH3, CH2, and CO that were possibly contributed from cellulose and hemicellulose were weaken or disappeared after carbonisation. It is obvious that the broad band in the region of 1300–1500cm−1 for the CNPS was due to the formation of aromatic rich compounds with CC functional group. Besides, the 1379cm−1 peak could be due to the OH in-plane bending and the 1560cm−1 could be due to the COO− asymmetric stretching. This suggests that the CNPs were with electron rich functional groups.

Major parameters governing the conversion efficiency of the orange peels into CNPs have been optimised. This can minimise the resources required for the conversion in line with the green and sustainable objectives, while able to obtain the maximum yield of CNPs. Particularly for this study, the fluorescence intensity was used as a semi-quantitative measurable variable due to its correlation with the amount of CNPs. CNPs were the only species that show fluorescence in the mixture and thus any increment of the intensity is assumed to reflect on the increasing amount of CNPs produced and vice versa.

The pyrolysis temperature was observed to have a direct influence on the carbonisation process. Temperatures below 200°C were not able to carbonise the peels completely within an hour and this can be clearly identified from the residue that was not homogenously black but still having spots of light yellow colour on the sample. The supernatant collected after the residue was grinded, sonicated and centrifuged showed significantly low fluorescence. The supernatant prepared in the same way only started to show good fluorescence when the residue used was carbonised at a temperature of 250°C. The fluorescence continued to rise for samples that were treated with higher temperatures until it showed an optimum at the temperature of 325°C (Fig. 3 A). Pyrolysis temperatures beyond 325°C have seen to cause significant drop in the fluorescence. From the physical eyes observation, the residues collected at these high temperatures were no longer black throughout, but rather ashy with light fluffy grey flakes. This was due to the excessive decomposition of the peels into ashes due to high temperature, which directly caused the drop in the amount of CNPs. The use of higher temperature to achieve optimum pyrolysis could be a disadvantage in term of energy efficiency, but as a whole, the process is clean, simple, and involves less toxic reagents. This is far better as compared to many others common organic syntheses of fluorophores.

Sufficient time for carbonisation is also required to ensure complete decomposition of the orange peels into rich carbon residues. In this study, different durations taken up to 4h for the pyrolysis were investigated. The result shows that longer time can promote better carbonisation but excessive pyrolysis duration will again lead to the formation of ashes that caused the drop in the intensity of fluorescence (Fig. 3B). At the set temperature of 325°C, the optimum time for pyrolysis was found to be around 2h, which it has been chosen as the optimum pyrolysis duration for this study.

In order to isolate the fluorescent CNPs, the residue after pyrolysis was grinded into powder, dispersed in water, sonicated, and finally the light yellowish supernatant was collected after centrifugation. In these series of processes, grinding of the carbon residue was to crush the residue mechanically into finer particles since only nano-sized particles will give fluorescence. Sonication was introduced to promote the reduction of the size of the particles into the nano-range size. Ultrasonic waves can lead to microjet, shock-wave impact, and promote of inter-particles collision, which all resulted in the reduction of particles size [29]. Sonication after an hour has improved the fluorescence yield of CNPs by 12.3%.

In this study, grinding has resulted in cracking of the carbon residue into smaller particles with wide distribution of sizes. The dispersion of this black power in water has caused the final mixture to be muddy and showed very low fluorescence. It was observed not all particles were solvated since some particles are comparatively large in size and can be seen even using naked eyes. Self-quenching can occur in this colloidal mixture and turned off the fluorescence intensity. Since the larger carbon particles was not fluorescing but instead promoted the self-quenching, centrifugation has been performed to sink these larger particles and later discarded. Smaller sized particles will remain dispersed in the supernatant and ready to be isolated for further studies. Fig. 4 clearly shows that the intensity of the mixture has increased drastically at least 3-folds after the centrifugation and reached plateau after 5min of centrifugation. The solution after centrifugation was yellowish, but very clear with no particles observed using naked eyes.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensing potential of heavy metal ions
ce:section.ce:para: Orange peels that were initially considered as agricultural waste have been successfully converted into highly fluorescing CNPs using the method established in this study. The optimum pyrolysis condition and the most efficient handling procedures have been identified. Following this success, the next intention was to investigate the potential of CNPs for optical sensing application. Some heavy metal ions were chosen as targeted analytes to be tested since they are of environmental and health concerns that require monitoring [30]. Besides, CNPs have showed some successes in detecting metal ions previously, although the CNPs were produced using different routes and starting precursors as compared to this study [31,32].

The analytical potential of CNPs has been evaluated by monitoring the change in the properties of the fluorescence when added with metal ions, either in the intensity or wavelength number. The conditions for the sensing were first optimised before the evaluation. It was found that the effect caused by the pH towards the intensity was not very significant. The general trend showed a slight enhancement in intensity as the pH condition moved from acidic to basic region (Fig. 5 ). This could lead to the deprotonation at the surface of the CNPs and can subsequently weaken the intermolecular hydrogen bonds that initially attracting the CNPs together [33]. Aggregation often causes self-quenching to take place and will turn off the fluorescence. Thus, the subsequent studies were performed in solution having pH in the range of 6–8. Higher pH was avoided since the presence of hydroxide could interact with most of the metal ions, forming precipitates. Besides pH, the amount of CNPs dispersed in system (ratio of CNPs to solvent) has also a direct effect on the intensity recorded. In this case, the use of 2.0vol/vol% of CNPs can achieve the highest intensity of the CNPs before addition of metal ions, while further amount added has caused the intensity reading to exceed the instrument scale.

Metal ions commonly encountered in the environment were chosen to be tested in this study, which consisted of Cu(II), Sn(II), Ni(II), Co(II), Pb(II), Ag(I) and Hg(II) ions. The study found that all the metal ions tested have caused no shifting on the emission peak wavelength, but instead showed different degrees of quenching toward the fluorescence intensity of the CNPs (Fig. 6 ). This was a positive sign that CNPs can be used for sensing on those selected metal ions after optimisation.

To evaluate the sensing capability, a case study has been performed by selecting Hg(II) and Sn(II) ions as target analytes. Micro-titration method has been employed by stepwise additions of the metal ions into the solution containing CNPs and the subsequent fluorescence intensities after each addition were recorded. It was obvious from the study that the intensity was quenched at a consistent trend but not linear (Fig. 7 ). All the experimental parameters and conditions were set identical for these two metal ions, which including the counter-ions of both the metal ions. However, the quenching trends were found to be different for the two metal ions, implying that the reduction in the intensity was due to the specific interaction of the metal ions with CNPs, while not by other means such as the counter-ions or the solvent system employed in this study.

The modelling of the signals to generate significant quantitative analytical output was performed in order to fully leverage on the potential of CNPs as a detection probe. Since the quenching trends were observed to be of concentration-dependent manner, standard Stern–Volmer model given by the following equation (Eq. (1)) was adopted for the data analysis:
(1)
F
0
/ F =
K
sv
[ C ] + 1
where F 0 and F are the fluorescence intensities recorded in absence and presence of metal ions respectively, [C] is the concentration of metal ions and K sv is the Stern–Volmer quenching constant.

Raw data recorded at 435nm via the micro-titration approach for both the metal ions were fed into Eq. (1) and linear trends have been observed within the concentration range studied (insert Fig. 7). The correlation coefficient, R 2 values for both the trends were comparatively high, indicating the Stern–Volmer has successfully modelled the data. The slopes were observed to be different and this can be explained by the different interaction affinities between the metal ions tested with CNPs. CNPs gave better sensitivity towards Sn(II) ions as compared to the Hg(II) ions, as directly indicated from the steeper slope of the plot generated from Sn(II) ions. The linear relationship can serve as calibration curve to identify of an unknown concentration of the specific metal ions by simply referring to the degree of quenching.

The sensitivity of the CNPs sensing probe for different metal ions can be evaluated using the limit of detection (LOD) as a scale, where LOD is the lowest concentration of which an analytical technique can achieve for a given analyte. For the Sn(II) and Hg(II) ions, the LODs were evaluated to be 0.21 and 1.20mM respectively. Using the same evaluation approach, the analytical characteristic for all the other ions tested in this study were evaluated as tabulated in Table 1 . This shows that the CNPs were able to detect all the metal ions tested with different degrees of sensitivity. As mentioned earlier, the origin of the fluorescence is yet to be understood fully and thus it will be difficult to identify the exact quenching mechanism observed in this study. However, it has been quite clear that carbon nanostructures have high absorption affinity towards metal ions [34,35], which means the metal ions will be at close contact with the carbon moiety. Such close distance of a quencher to a fluorescent species usually will disrupt the fluorescence transition to other non-radiative pathways, which will be seen as quenching. Furthermore, precipitates was observed in some of the sample after the addition with metal ions, especially with Sn(II) and Pb(II). This showed the formation of complex between the metal ions and the CNPs. Thus, most probably static quenching was taking place via common mechanisms such as the fluorescence resonance energy transfer [36] and the electron transfer [37].

Within the scope of this study, it was found that CNPs showed cross-reactivity towards various metal ions tested, which has reduced the robustness of the proposed probe to detect a specific metal ions within a multi-analytes sample. However, the probe will be still useful and relevant to detect single metal ion species in a sample that has been pre-separated from other interfering ions. The application potential is still wide since CNPs showed low in toxicity and can be easily prepared from naturally available resources at a low cost. There will be less issue of contamination and side effects during and after application, especially in sensitive areas such as for biomedical diagnosis, environmental monitoring and food analysis. This is definitely an advantage compared to quantum dots. If deemed necessary, further surface modification on the surface of CNPs will be an alternative to introduce specific functional groups to CNPs, which it is now driven to interact specifically with an intended metal ions. With such approach, specificity of the CNPs can be improved significantly. For instance, Helena and co-workers [38] have developed an optical fibre sensor specifically for Hg(II) ions by surface modified CNPs with polyethylene glycol and N-acetyl-l-cysteine. However, further engineering of CNPs with functional groups is out of the scope of this work and would not be reported here.

This study has successfully demonstrated an innovative and facile approach of converting orange peels into fluorescent CNPs, where orange peels are considered as agricultural waste with low commodity value. This work employed a greener approach during the synthesis by avoiding excess and toxic chemical reagents as starting precursors, besides a clean synthesis that produced low or no harmful side chemical products. The CNPs could serve as a cleaner alternative to existing fluorophores that have less focus on sustainability in the synthesis process. The optimum condition for the conversion has been identified and the isolated CNPs were demonstrated with high potential to be used as detection probe for various metal ions.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This project was supported by the Faculty of Engineering, Computing and Science, and the Swinburne Sarawak Research Centre for Sustainable Technologies, both under the Swinburne University of Technology Sarawak Campus. Financial support was obtained from the Swinburne Sarawak Research Grant (SSRG-2-5509). The authors would like to acknowledge the support by all the staff, technicians, postgraduates and collaborators that have contributed to this study.

ce:other-ref.ce:textref: H. Shengliang, G. Yan, T. Ruixue, Synthesis and size control of carbon quantum dots by tailoring laser parameters, Electronics and Optoelectronics (ICEOE), 2011 International Conference on 2011, pp. V2-283-V282-285.
